text: A small loop of the ileum is adhered to the lateral pelvic wall, and the inflamed appendix is visible behind it, containing a fecolith.
The surgeon mobilizes the intestinal loop to access the base of the inflamed appendix, revealing the spermatic vessel adhered near the triangle of dome.

In the right hypochondrium, a grasper stretches the ileum near the fat pad, ensuring the serosa remains intact. The surgeon uses a harmonic device with the Teflon jaw positioned towards the spermatic vessel to perform adhesiolysis, separating the intestinal loop from the pectoral fold.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers near the peritoneum to prevent collateral thermal spread from affecting the adjacent intestinal lobe during dissection.
The surgeon identifies the spermatic vessels below and carefully stretches the peritoneum to perform adhesiolysis, ensuring minimal collateral thermal spread.
The surgeon has successfully separated the adhered loop of ileum from the lateral pelvic wall, providing clear access to the inflamed appendix, which is visible behind the ileum.
The right hypochondrium port grasps the appendix, applying anterosuperior traction to reveal the base of the appendix, which shows some adhesion.
The surgeon uses the harmonic scalpel to dissect bands of adhesion, carefully positioning the Teflon jaw towards the cecum to prevent vibration transfer to the cecum.
The surgeon identifies the appendicular vessel and begins the process of separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully dissects to reach the base of the appendix, ensuring a knot is tied close to the cecum to prevent incomplete appendectomy, especially in cases with fecolith presence.
The surgeon performs adhesiolysis to mobilize the cecum, which was adhered to the lateral pelvic wall, facilitating access to the base of the appendix.
The surgeon applies medial traction to the appendix, providing better access to the peritoneum. The harmonic scalpel is used to cut through the folds of tissue.
The surgeon has reached the base of the appendix, revealing the genia coli area, which appears free of adhesions.

The surgeon uses a Mishra's knot, inserting a grasper into the loop to catch the appendix and transfer the loop. The tip of the knot pusher is maneuvered to reach the base of the appendix, nearly touching the cecum.
The surgeon transfers the knot as close as possible to the cecum and tightens it securely, ensuring the knot is firmly positioned at the base of the appendix.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to separate the appendix, ensuring the mucosa is cauterized. An 18mm diameter structure is tightened multiple times before the suture is cut with scissors.
The surgeon positions the harmonic scalpel with its concavity facing the appendix, using the heat generated to disinfect any fecal matter present.

The surgeon ensures the Teflon jaw of the harmonic scalpel is positioned upwards while applying anterior traction to prevent contact with the suture. Care is taken to avoid the tip of the harmonic touching the cecum to prevent potential puncture.
The surgeon carefully separates the appendix from its base in two to three attempts, using precise movements to ensure the appendix is detached without damaging surrounding structures.
The surgeon completes the separation of the appendix from its base using a harmonic device. The appendix is carefully held to prevent contamination of the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon uses a commercially available endo bag, known as an endo catch, to contain the appendix. The endo bag is inserted through a ten millimeter port.

The surgeon opens the mouth of the endo catch bag, which features a self-retaining metal ring, and carefully places the appendix inside. The grasper is used to push the appendix further into the bag, ensuring it does not fall out.
The surgeon pulls the persisting suture to close the endo bag containing the appendix, preparing to remove the bag from the surgical site.
The surgeon places an artery forceps by the side of the endo bag and gently stretches the wound to facilitate the removal of the endo bag containing the appendix.
The surgeon uses suture passers to close the ten millimeter port wound with number one Vicryl sutures, preventing hernia formation after the endo bag has stretched the wound.
The surgeon tightens the closure to stop the gas leak, confirming the seal. Betadine is applied over the base of the appendix, and excess betadine is suctioned out.
Betadine is applied to disinfect the base of the appendix, followed by suction to remove excess solution.
